text: Nexus is a contextual multi-armed bandit.
Its objective is to maximize a scalar reward derived from engagement — opens, conversions, donations, and reading-plan completions — across push, email, and in-app.
caption: maximize a scalar engagement reward

text: Over the next ten minutes we'll walk the full pipeline, from a user becoming a vector to proving the whole thing works.
Start with representation. Every user in Nexus is a point in a ten-dimensional feature space.
The dimensions fall into two bands: zero through five are behavioral, six through nine are semantic.
caption: behavioral [0–5] · semantic [6–9]

text: The behavioral band. Dimension zero is push conversion rate; dimension one is email — deliberately not normalized against each other, because absolute channel preference matters.
caption: [0] push rate   [1] email rate

text: Dimensions two, three, and four are temporal ratios — the share of engagement in the morning, the evening, and on weekends.
caption: [2,3,4] morning · evening · weekend

text: They're independent windows, so they don't sum to one. Dimension five is the overall conversion rate.
caption: [5] overall conversion rate

text: The semantic band, synced from YouVersion through Hightouch. Dimension six is a recency score — a linear decay over ninety days since last open.
caption: [6] recency = 1 − min(1, days/90)

text: Dimension seven is the giving tier — zero, one-half, or one. Dimension eight is spiritual depth, a composite of five reading and prayer signals.
caption: [7] giving tier   [8] spiritual depth

text: Dimension nine is engagement frequency, log-scaled sends per week. Every dimension is normalized to roughly zero to one.
caption: [9] frequency · all ~[0, 1]

text: This replaced an older forty-four dimension layout, where correlated histogram bins dominated the distance metric.
caption: 44 dims → 10 interpretable dims

text: Collapsing them into a handful of interpretable ratios gives a denser vector, better convergence, and cleaner arm separation.
Distance matters because Nexus compares users by cosine similarity, not Euclidean distance.
caption: cosine, not Euclidean

text: Cosine measures the angle between two vectors — invariant to magnitude. Cosine distance is one minus that.
caption: d(a,b) = 1 − cos(a, b)

text: New users have an all-zero vector, undefined under cosine, so they're skipped until their first conversion lands.
caption: cold start: all-zero vector → skipped

text: Now clustering. Nexus discovers personas with k-means plus plus over cosine distance.
caption: k-means++ initialization

text: Pick the first centroid at random, then sample each next one with probability proportional to its squared distance from the nearest chosen centroid.
caption: P(i) = D(i)² / Σ D(j)²

text: That spreads the seeds across the space and converges faster.
Then it iterates. The assignment step puts each user in the cluster whose centroid maximizes cosine similarity.
caption: assign: argmax cosine similarity

text: The update step recomputes each centroid as the mean of its members.
caption: update: centroid = mean of members

text: It repeats until no assignment changes, usually within fifteen to thirty iterations.
But how many personas? Nexus doesn't fix k. It runs every k in a range and scores each with the silhouette coefficient.
caption: s(i) = (b − a) / max(a, b)

text: It keeps the k with the highest mean silhouette, provided it clears a floor of zero point two five.
caption: keep best k · floor 0.25

text: Initialization is stochastic, so it runs five stability runs per k and keeps the best. A rule layer can then override for high-signal archetypes.
caption: 5 stability runs + rule overrides

text: The payoff of clustering: each persona gets its own independent set of arm statistics.
caption: one bandit per persona

text: Variant A can beat Variant B for one segment while the reverse holds for another.
Now the decision layer — three algorithms. The default is Thompson Sampling.
caption: Algorithm 1 · Thompson Sampling

text: Each arm carries a Beta distribution: alpha, accumulated weighted success, and beta, accumulated failure.
caption: Beta(α, β) · E[θ] = α/(α+β)

text: Draw one sample from every arm's Beta and send the highest. That single rule is both exploration and exploitation.
caption: draw all · send argmax

text: New arms are initialized pessimistically, at Beta one, thirty — an expected value near three percent, matching real push click-through.
caption: Beta(1, 30) ≈ 3%

text: Beta samples come from two Gamma samples using Marsaglia-Tsang with a Box-Muller normal — order-one amortized, no math library.
caption: Marsaglia-Tsang + Box-Muller

text: Selection can also be deflated by a recency penalty, so one variant doesn't dominate a single user's history.
The second algorithm is LinUCB — a contextual bandit. It conditions on the feature vector that Thompson Sampling ignores.
caption: Algorithm 2 · LinUCB

text: Each arm keeps an inverse design matrix A-inverse, ten by ten, and a reward vector b. The weights are theta equals A-inverse times b.
caption: θ = A⁻¹ b

text: The score is theta-transpose x, the exploit term, plus alpha times the square root of x-transpose A-inverse x, the exploration bonus.
caption: score = θᵀx + α·√(xᵀA⁻¹x)

text: That bonus is large when the context points somewhere the arm hasn't seen, so unfamiliar users get explored.
caption: unfamiliar context → uncertainty bonus

text: A full inverse would be order d-cubed every step; the Sherman-Morrison formula does a rank-one update in order d-squared.
caption: Sherman-Morrison: O(d³) → O(d²)

text: Use Thompson Sampling when the bandit is persona-segmented; reach for LinUCB for fine-grained, within-segment personalization.
The third is Epsilon-Greedy, the legacy fallback. With probability epsilon, pick a random arm; otherwise pick the highest win rate.
caption: Algorithm 3 · ε-greedy

text: Simple and auditable, but its exploration is blind, and epsilon decays toward a floor of one percent.
caption: ε decays → floor 1%

text: Next: how engagement becomes reward. Goals are tiered — best plus ten, down to disabling push at minus ten.
caption: tiers: +10 best … −10 worst

text: The raw reward is the tier base times a weight, divided by a hundred and clamped to minus one through one.
caption: r = clamp(base × weight / 100, −1, 1)

text: The update is asymmetric: a positive reward adds its value to alpha, while any failure adds exactly one to beta.
caption: r>0: α += r   else: β += 1

text: Statistics live at two levels — a shared persona prior and a per-user posterior — blended by observation count.
caption: persona prior + per-user posterior

text: Attribution connects send to outcome: forty-eight hours standard, thirty days for long-horizon goals, ninety days retroactive for disabling push.
caption: 48h · 30d · 90d retroactive

text: And after each update, alpha and beta both decay by zero point nine nine — a half-life of about sixty-nine updates.
caption: α, β ×= 0.99 · half-life ≈ 69

text: Timing matters too. From each user's last-seen time, Nexus derives a preferred send hour, then schedules ten minutes earlier.
caption: preferred hour − 10 min

text: If there's no signal or the time has passed, it falls back to a default hour and advances to tomorrow, because Braze rejects past timestamps.
A timing lottery then admits only users whose preferred time is near the next batch, spreading sends across the day.
caption: timing lottery spreads sends

text: Finally — does it work? Lift is the treatment rate minus a uniform-random baseline, over that baseline.
caption: Lift = (CTRₜ − CTR_c) / CTR_c

text: A two-proportion z-test pools the proportions and rejects the null when the absolute z exceeds one point nine six.
caption: reject H₀ when |Z| > 1.96

text: But Thompson Sampling concentrates traffic on winners, breaking the equal-split assumption, so wide intervals at low sample sizes are shown honestly.
caption: let CIs narrow — don't poll early

text: That's the full pipeline. Represent, cluster, choose, reward, decay, schedule, and measure — every hour, across every channel.
caption: represent · cluster · choose · reward · decay · schedule · measure

text: In production, a thirty-four percent lift over random. That's Nexus.
caption: +34% lift in production
